title: Funding Ask
note: No dollar amount appears here because the amount depends on team size and cloud/AI costs (Phase 1/2) and hardware costs (Phase 3) that discovery work in each phase determines. What appears is the STRUCTURE of the ask, the use of funds, and the milestone each tranche unlocks — now organised around building and launching the free app first, layering premium monetisation, and only then piloting hardware. Fill amounts in as each phase's discovery work completes.

the_framing.principle: Raise in tranches against de-risking milestones, phase by phase, not a lump sum against a revenue projection.
the_framing.why: This is now a software company first (with a real, fast path to a launched product and usage data) and a hardware company second (with the same long validation timeline the original plan described). The credible structure is: a small first raise to build and launch the free app and observe real user behaviour, a second raise to prove the monetisation layer converts, and a third to fund the hardware pilot once the software business has either funded itself partially or proven the customer relationship the hardware sale depends on.
the_framing.the_honest_pitch: We are not asking you to believe a subscriber-growth forecast or a drone-deployment forecast. We are asking you to fund the removal of a specific risk at each stage, watch us either clear it or kill that layer of the company on it, and price the next round on what you learn.

the_tranche_structure.purpose: Build and launch the Phase 1 free app, and observe whether beginner/smallholder farmers actually use it.
use_of_funds: Mobile/web app development (guide, setup assistant, water-quality logging, feeding schedules, disease-ID, community, learning modules)
Cloud hosting and AI inference infrastructure, sized for a free tier
Community management and content/education production for organic growth
Farmer discovery to validate the free-tier feature set before and during build
milestones_that_unlock_the_next_tranche: App launched publicly on at least one app store.
A measured activation rate (farm setup completed, at least one log entered) at a credible level — see unit-economics.json#/phase_1_foundation_economics for the design target.
Evidence that organic/community channels can acquire users without paid marketing at meaningful volume.
A real, if small, base of retained weekly-active users generating logged water-quality and production data.
the_tranche_structure.the_kill_criteria: If, after a genuine organic-growth effort, activation and retention are poor even at zero price, the honest conclusion is that the free tier is not solving a real problem for this audience as designed, and the company should re-scope the product before raising further — not proceed to build a paid tier or a hardware pilot on top of an unused base.
the_tranche_structure.why_this_is_the_smart_first_raise: It is the cheapest phase in the entire three-phase plan and it produces the company's first real data of any kind — the first users, the first retention numbers, the first evidence for or against the freemium thesis. An investor who funds only this is exposed to a small amount against the single largest and fastest-testable uncertainty in the model: will anyone use this.

the_tranche_structure.purpose: Layer in the Phase 2 premium subscription, marketplace, and consultation features, and prove the free-to-paid conversion funnel converts at a viable rate.
use_of_funds: Premium analytics and predictive-health feature development, trained on Phase 1 logged data
Marketplace build and supply-side partner onboarding (feed, equipment, buyers)
Consultation marketplace build and expert recruitment
milestones_that_unlock_the_next_tranche: A measured free-to-paid conversion rate, replacing the generic 2-5% assumption in unit-economics.json with a real number.
Measured ARPU, churn, and gross margin on the paying subscriber base.

Evidence (or disconfirmation) of the assumed synergy that Phase 2 subscribers convert into Phase 3 hardware leads at lower CAC than cold enterprise sales — see unit-economics.json#/the_cross_phase_synergy_and_its_risk.
At least early marketplace and/or consultation transaction volume, even if small.
the_tranche_structure.note: This is the tranche that answers whether AquaVision is a viable subscription-and-marketplace software business at all, independent of whether the Phase 3 hardware layer ever ships. If conversion and retention do not hold up here, the honest move is to fix or re-scope the software business before funding hardware capex on top of it.

the_tranche_structure.purpose: Fund the Phase 3 hardware pilot — drone, dock, IoT probes, PondTwin digital twin, and enterprise dashboard — for commercial and enterprise operators, once the software layer has either proven itself or shown a credible warm-lead pipeline into this segment.
use_of_funds: Drone/dock/sonde BOM finalisation and subsystem prototyping (winch, sensor payload)
Field validation over a full seasonal cycle
milestones: A validated payload and mass budget closing with margin (unchanged kill criterion from the original hardware plan).
Winch reliability and profile-vs-reference-sonde agreement demonstrated (unchanged from the original plan — the moment the core hardware claim becomes true or false).
A validation dataset across one full seasonal cycle, with academic partner review.
The first paying Phase 3 hardware customers with renewal intent, and measured (not modeled) Phase 3 unit economics.
BVLOS obtained, or the model honestly re-based on VLOS economics.
the_tranche_structure.note: By this raise, the company should have evidence on BOTH layers — real software usage/revenue metrics from Phase 1/2, and real hardware validation data from Phase 3 field work — not just a plan for either. If it does not, the earlier tranches did their job by revealing that.

use_of_funds_principles: The single most valuable early dollar goes to shipping and launching the free app, because it is the cheapest possible way to learn whether the core premise — free, low-friction access converts beginner farmers into engaged users — holds at all.

The second goes to instrumenting and measuring the free-to-paid conversion funnel honestly, because freemium conversion (not hardware engineering) is now the single most load-bearing untested number in the whole model.

Do NOT spend meaningfully on hardware tooling or BOM commitments before the Phase 2 gate shows the software business has real traction, or at minimum before Phase 0-equivalent hardware discovery (payload arithmetic, farmer WTP for automated monitoring) closes on paper — tooling an aircraft, or funding an enterprise sales team, ahead of either kind of evidence is the most expensive avoidable mistake available.

Community and content spend in Phase 1 should be weighted toward earning organic distribution, not paid acquisition — paid acquisition working around the stated channel assumption would itself be a signal that assumption failed, not a fix for it.

the_things_we_volunteer: Zero app users, zero revenue, no team hired, as of today.
The freemium conversion rate used in every downstream model is a generic software-industry heuristic, not a measured number for this product or market.
The Phase 2-to-Phase 3 'warm lead' synergy the phased story depends on is an assumption, not a demonstrated mechanism — see unit-economics.json#/the_cross_phase_synergy_and_its_risk.
The Phase 3 hardware layer carries every open risk the original hardware-first plan named: nothing has flown, TRL 3, no BVLOS authorisation, unit economics unproven and density-dependent.

The customer who needs the platform most (the smallholder) is also the one least able to pay for anything beyond the free tier — Phase 1 solves the access problem, not the revenue problem, for that segment.
what_we_are_honest_about_with_investors.why_volunteer_all_of_this: Because every one of these is discoverable in diligence, and handing them over with the mitigations attached is cheaper than being caught with them. A team that leads with 'here is the number we borrowed from a generic benchmark and here is why it might be wrong for this market' has more credibility than one that presents a confident subscriber-growth model built on the same borrowed number without saying so.
what_we_are_honest_about_with_investors.the_counterintuitive_bet: This repository is built on the wager that rigorous honesty is more fundable than polished optimism, for the right investor — a wager that applies exactly as much to a freemium conversion assumption as it did to a hardware BOM. It will lose the investors who want the hockey-stick, software or hardware. It is designed to win the ones worth having.

what_the_money_buys_the_investor.the_return_thesis: A phased position in the aquaculture-management category: first, a low-cost-to-build software platform with a genuine acquisition advantage among an underserved smallholder segment; second, a subscription-and-marketplace revenue layer on top of that base; third, a defensible hardware-as-a-service position (accumulated site data, local regulatory relationships, and the winch/calibration engineering) in the observability layer of an industry that just became the majority of the world's aquatic animal production and is still growing.
what_the_money_buys_the_investor.the_honest_risk: Each layer of this thesis is a plan, not an asset, at this stage, and the layers are only as connected as the untested cross-phase synergy assumption holds. The bet is on the team's ability to execute all three phases in sequence, converting real usage data into real revenue into a real hardware business, faster than a better-capitalised entrant in either the software or hardware category. That is a real risk and it is the risk the investor is actually pricing.
